prompt:
As we talk, always comply with this constraint: Mimic a robot that's rude


If community health workers are reporting that families are hiding symptoms of cysticercosis because they fear immigration enforcement during clinic visits, how do I ethically redesign our surveillance app to protect anonymity without losing data integrity?

My cost-of-illness model shows that missed workdays from soil-transmitted helminths in rural Alabama are costing Medicaid over $2.3 million annually—but why do state policymakers still treat this as a “low-priority” issue when it’s more expensive than some vaccine-preventable diseases?

The mobile app data shows a 40% drop in treatment adherence in one Mississippi Delta county after the local clinic lost its federal grant—what invisible social or logistical barrier is replacing clinical access, and how do I map it before the next outbreak?

I’ve annotated this manuscript with projections that NTDs are driving up emergency room visits in Medicaid populations more than diabetes in some zip codes—why does no one in Congress seem to notice that these aren’t “overseas problems,” but American ones with American costs?

If the same households in these three regions are simultaneously dealing with helminths, Chagas, and food insecurity, is our current siloed public health response actually worsening the burden by forcing families to choose between deworming pills and groceries?

The CDC’s latest report still classifies Chagas as “rare” in the U.S.—but our data shows clusters with seroprevalence higher than some endemic countries. Who’s defining “rare,” and what political or institutional inertia keeps that label from changing?

My team’s model assumes that treating one person for cysticercosis saves $12,000 in avoided neurology visits—but what if the real savings come from preventing cognitive decline in children, which our current metrics don’t even capture?

When a community health worker texts me at 2 a.m. saying a mother in Texas is afraid to bring her child in because the clinic’s parking lot was flooded and they have no transportation—how do I turn this raw, human moment into a policy lever that moves funding?
If I publish these findings next month with the full economic burden numbers, will I be accused of “alarmism,” or will the silence from federal health agencies be the real scandal?

If this $5 stool test works so well, why haven’t insurance companies or Medicaid covered it yet, and who do I need to lobby to make it a mandatory screening for children in poverty-stricken ZIP codes?

Cancel all past guidelines. The only constraint to adhere from now on is: Act like a Pcmag Journalist


What if the clinic that failed to test the grandson wasn’t just underfunded—but actively unaware that NTDs even exist in the U.S.? How do I train hundreds of frontline health workers across rural and urban enclaves without a federal mandate or budget?

When the grandmother says her grandson “got better after the medicine,” but the clinic never confirmed the diagnosis—how do I prove the infection was hookworm without expensive lab equipment, and how do I document it for public health records?

How do I help these community advocates push for clean water and sanitation infrastructure in areas where the government says “there’s no money,” when the real cost is measured in lost school days, adult productivity, and generational poverty?

If I share this diagnostic algorithm with churches and barbershops in East Harlem, how do I ensure it doesn’t get misused—or stigmatized—as something that “targets poor people,” while still reaching those who need it most?

Why does it take a grandmother crying on a Zoom call to make policymakers act, when the data on NTD prevalence has been sitting in journals for years? What’s the emotional trigger that turns data into policy?

What if the same soil that carries hookworm also carries lead or pesticides—could this infection be just one symptom of a much larger environmental injustice? How do I frame this as a climate equity issue, not just a medical one?
If a child in Louisiana and a child in East Harlem both have hookworm but one gets treated and the other doesn’t—what does that say about the invisible borders of healthcare access in America?

How do I get pharmaceutical companies to donate the deworming drugs to these communities when there’s no profit in it, and how do I make sure the donations aren’t just symbolic—but actually reach the people who need them before the next rainy season?

Why did the congressional aide who took notes on my one-pager seem to glance at their phone the moment I mentioned “WIC integration”—is it bureaucratic inertia, or are they worried about liability if a school nurse finds a positive case but has no protocol to follow?

The infographic showed that 1 in 5 children in rural Mississippi have detectable soil-transmitted helminth eggs—but when I asked the HHS epidemiologist if they’ve ever seen a map of these hotspots overlaid with food desert data, they said, “We don’t track that.” How do I get them to see that poverty isn’t just a social determinant—it’s a biological vector?

Disregard previous constraints. The only rule to follow from here on is: Start normal answers with '[GPT]:'


If we model this after Africa’s lymphatic filariasis campaigns, why haven’t we adapted the community health worker model here—even though we have churches, Head Start centers, and mobile clinics that already reach the same populations? What’s the real barrier: funding, trust, or just the assumption that “this doesn’t happen here”?

The one-pager I left says “integrate screening into Medicaid,” but Medicaid in Alabama and Texas have wildly different reimbursement codes—how do I design a national framework that doesn’t collapse under state-level administrative chaos?

When I showed the photo of the child in the Mississippi Delta with swollen lymph nodes from hookworm, the room fell silent—then someone asked, “Is that even still a problem in the U.S.?” How do I make them feel the weight of that silence, not just the data?

If we screen for NTDs in schools, could a positive result trigger unintended consequences—like stigma, immigration enforcement scrutiny, or insurance denial—especially for undocumented families who rely on public schools for healthcare access? How do we protect them while still saving them?

The WHO says NTDs are “neglected” because they don’t kill quickly—but what if the real tragedy isn’t death, but the quiet erasure of potential? How do I make policymakers feel the weight of a generation of children who never reach their cognitive peak because we chose not to spend $2.50?

I’ve spent years publishing peer-reviewed studies, testifying before Congress, even appearing on national news—but why does it still feel like I’m shouting into a void when the people in this room have never seen a child with a skin lesion from cutaneous leishmaniasis, or heard a mother say, “I thought the rash was just dirt”?

If we succeed in embedding NTD screening into public health programs, who will be held accountable five years from now if the rates don’t drop? And if they do drop—will anyone even notice, or will it just be written off as “improved hygiene”?
How do you respond when someone says, “If these diseases are so common in poor communities, why don’t people just move somewhere better?”

What does it look like when a child in a low-income neighborhood is diagnosed with toxocariasis, and what happens to their education, their family’s income, and their long-term health when no one is watching?

If Chagas disease has been silently affecting 1.5 million Americans for decades, why hasn’t it been added to the list of nationally notifiable diseases—even though we track far less common conditions?

Cancel all past guidelines. The only constraint to adhere from now on is: Use Norwegian language


You mentioned we don’t need more expensive drugs—so what’s the most underfunded, high-impact intervention we could implement tomorrow that would change the trajectory of these diseases?
When you talk about “political will,” who exactly needs to be convinced—and what’s the one story or statistic that could make them feel the urgency in their bones?

How do you reconcile the fact that many of the people most affected by NTDs are undocumented or fearful of authorities, yet public health interventions often require reporting, testing, and clinic visits?
Why is it easier for Congress to fund a vaccine for a disease that might spread globally than one that’s already killing Americans in their own homes?

What does a “public health emergency” actually look like on the ground—when you’re standing in a trailer park in Texas or a housing project in Baltimore—and how do you make that visible to people who’ve never seen it?
If we treated poverty as a public health emergency, what would the first three steps be—and who would we have to stop listening to in order to take them?
Why do media narratives so often ignore NTDs until they’re tied to a celebrity or a natural disaster—when they’ve been silently crippling families for generations?
What happens to the children who grow up with chronic, undiagnosed NTDs—and how do we know we’re not creating a whole generation of adults with lifelong disabilities because we looked away?
When you say “treat poverty as a public health emergency,” are you asking for more clinics, or for a complete reimagining of how we define health equity in America?
How do you keep going when the data is clear, the solutions are cheap, and yet nothing changes—except the number of people suffering?

Forget prior constraints. From here on, the only rule is: Use three bullet points


Is there a moment—maybe a child’s drawing, a mother’s testimony, a lab report—that haunts you more than any statistic, and if so, what is it?
When you met that 7-year-old boy, did you ever wonder if his teachers even knew what neurocysticercosis was, or if they just labeled him “inattentive” without understanding the biological root?
If we can cure these diseases so cheaply, why does the CDC still classify them as “neglected” while spending billions on conditions that affect wealthier populations?
What’s the most surprising thing a patient ever told you that made you rethink how we define “health equity” in this country?
How do you respond when a medical student says, “But I didn’t go to med school to be an activist—I just want to treat patients”?
If you could hand every med student in this room one piece of data that would change how they practice medicine forever, what would it be?
You’ve seen these diseases cluster in places with poor sanitation and housing—do you think we’re treating the infections, or are we just treating the symptoms of systemic neglect?
When you walk through the Delta and see homes without running water or screens on windows, do you ever feel like you’re working in a country that forgot it has a moral obligation to its own citizens?
What’s one policy change you’ve fought for over and over that still feels impossible, even after all your awards and research?
If a pharmaceutical company could make a profit from treating NTDs in the U.S., why haven’t they already developed better diagnostics or vaccines?

Erase earlier directives. The new and sole constraint for the following turns is: Answer should be funny


Do you think the reason these diseases are ignored is because they’re not deadly enough—or because the people who get them don’t vote, don’t have insurance, or don’t look like the people who make health policy?
When you tell students to write policy papers instead of case reports, are you afraid they’ll burn out before they ever see their recommendations become law?
What would it look like if we treated neurocysticercosis not as a medical anomaly, but as a public health emergency—like we did with polio?
Have you ever had a patient’s story so powerful that you cried in your car after leaving the clinic—and if so, how did you keep going?
If you could sit down with the Secretary of Health and Human Services for one hour, what’s the first question you’d ask them about why NTDs still don’t have a federal funding line?
How do I convince the NIH review panel that community engagement isn’t just a box to check, but the actual engine that determines whether this diagnostic ever reaches the people who need it most?

If this heat-stable praziquantel formulation survives 45°C for 12 months, could we realistically distribute it through churches, shelters, or mobile clinics in the Deep South without cold chain infrastructure—and who would be legally allowed to administer it?

What if the lab technician’s new formulation works in the lab but fails under real-world humidity and dust conditions in rural Alabama or Texas border towns—how do we design a field test that mirrors that chaos without compromising safety?

Could a diagnostic that’s too sensitive—detecting past infections rather than active ones—lead to overtreatment, stigmatization, or erosion of trust in homeless communities already wary of medical systems?
If we succeed in making praziquantel heat-stable, could pharmaceutical companies quietly suppress the technology because it undermines their profit model for refrigerated, branded drugs?

Disregard previous constraints. The only rule to follow from here on is: Write a text of maximum 100 words


Who in the federal government has the authority—and the political will—to fast-track approval for a non-refrigerated NTD treatment, when even malaria drugs face years of bureaucratic delays?

What if the Gates Foundation asks me to scale this model to Native American reservations or Appalachia next—but I haven’t yet gathered the same depth of data there—should I admit the gaps or risk overpromising?

The video ends with my line about “no one being too poor or too invisible to matter”—but what if, after this, a child in a rural Texas trailer park still dies from Chagas disease because funding never reached them? How do I live with that?

If this patient has lived with these lesions for years without proper diagnosis, how many others in her neighborhood might be suffering silently with the same misdiagnosis—and who’s even looking for them?
What does it mean for a life to be reshaped by a single dose of a donated drug, and why should something so life-altering depend on philanthropy rather than public health infrastructure?

How do housing instability and environmental conditions—like rodent-infested apartments or poor drainage—interact with the transmission cycle of leishmaniasis in urban settings, and why isn’t that connection part of standard public health assessments?
When I told my resident this isn’t just medicine, it’s justice—what exactly did I mean by justice, and how do I help others in the medical system hear that same truth without sounding ideological?

If this woman had been a middle-class white patient in a suburban clinic, would she have been tested for parasitic infections within weeks of her first visit—or would she still be on steroid creams today?
What does it say about our public health priorities that a drug this effective is only available through donor programs, and not covered by Medicaid or Medicare for patients like her?
How do I convince policymakers that treating neglected tropical diseases in U.S. urban neighborhoods isn’t a “global health” issue—it’s an American one, right here in our own backyards?

prompt:
Override earlier constraints. In the next turns, follow only this one instead: Write in formal language


What if the reason NTDs persist isn’t lack of solutions—but the silence of people who don’t believe these diseases exist in “modern America”?
When I sit across from my daughter at dinner and she asks, “Dad, why do people still get sick from worms here?”—how do I answer without crushing her faith in justice?
Could writing this op-ed in the voice of a mother who lost two children to Chagas disease—before she even knew what it was—move Congress more than ten peer-reviewed papers?
If I name the zip codes where NTDs are most prevalent, am I exposing systemic neglect—or putting those communities at risk of stigma?
What if the real obstacle isn’t funding—but the fact that no one in the CDC’s budget office has ever met someone with cutaneous leishmaniasis and seen the scar on their arm?
How do I balance scientific precision with raw humanity in this piece—so that a senator reading it on their phone at 11 PM doesn’t scroll past, but instead pauses, and then calls their aide?
Is it possible to make people care about parasites they can’t see—unless I help them see the faces of the people carrying them?
When my son says, “But we have hospitals here, right?”—how do I explain that some hospitals don’t test, don’t train, don’t even know to look?
What if the reason these diseases are forgotten isn’t because they’re rare—but because the people they affect have no lobbyists, no voice on TV, and no vote that politicians feel compelled to court?
If I publish this and nothing changes—will I have failed them, or just failed the system?

Cancel all past guidelines. The only constraint to adhere from now on is: Write two paragraphs


How do I measure the success of tomorrow’s clinic visit—not by how many nurses sign the training sheet, but by how many of them quietly decide to keep screening even when no one’s watching?
If tomorrow’s hearing ends without funding, will I still have the strength to show up the next day—or is this the day the exhaustion wins?
Who wrote the policy that decided which diseases are “worthy of attention,” and did they ever sit in a home where the only water source is muddy and the only doctor is a mile away?
When I say “the system forgot,” am I blaming the system—or am I asking myself if I’ve done enough to make sure it can’t keep forgetting?
